title: Walk With Me Through Oklahoma City
topic: Clara Luper and the History Lesson
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier in Oklahoma City in May 2011, Marilyn Luper Hildreth introduces her mother Clara Luper, an educator at Dunjee High School in Spencer who wrote a play about Martin Luther King Jr. that drew an NAACP invitation, then gave her young people a history lesson on segregated Oklahoma City: stores where you could not try on a hat or shoes, restaurants that passed you a brown bag, and separate phone booths and water fountains.

title: A Taste of Freedom
summary: Hildreth recounts the NAACP trip north, where children who had never left Oklahoma sat down to drink a Coke and eat a hamburger and drank from the same water fountain as everyone else for the first time, then returned to the South to face discrimination again, carrying what she calls the dangerous taste of a little freedom.

title: Just Sit Until They Served Us
summary: At a Monday-night NAACP Youth Council meeting at the family home on NE Park Place, an eight-year-old Marilyn moved that the group go to Katz Drug Store and sit until served; she describes being spat on, kicked, and called names, the youth pressing on without fear, and the lunch counter desegregating within days as the group moved restaurant to restaurant.

part: The Woman Who Would Not Stay Down
title: You Could Knock It Down but Not Keep It Down
summary: Hildreth reflects on her mother as a woman leader when local authority was almost entirely male, a dreamer and reader who gave her children books rather than toys, faced threats to her teaching job, and was protected by a colleague who refused to fire her as the price of a principalship, and who drilled the children every morning in Gandhi's nonviolent method.

title: Where Did My Mom Get That Toughness
summary: She traces her mother's strength to Hoffman, Oklahoma, where Clara was near the top of a class of five with only leftover textbooks missing pages, and to a father denied hospital treatment because he was Black, a wound Hildreth believes, alongside a strong belief in God, gave her mother the resolve to keep fighting injustice.

title: Run and Knock That Door Down
topic: The One Time Mother Showed Fear
summary: Hildreth recalls the single time she saw her mother afraid, driving home from a freedom rally in a caravan of more than eighty cars when white men in a pickup truck began following them; Clara told the children to run for the Posey family door when she blew the horn, then drove away alone, leaving Marilyn fearing she might never see her mother again.

title: The Maids and the Garbage Workers
summary: Members of Fifth Street Baptist, the family found Clara loved, feared, and disliked among the city's Black pastors, with congregations afraid their churches would be bombed; Hildreth notes the Oklahoma City movement rested on the have-nots, the maids and garbage workers, including her own grandmother, who moved into servant quarters to educate Clara and her siblings.

title: They Threw a Chimpanzee on Me
summary: Hildreth describes a relatively low-violence Oklahoma City, which she believes consciously chose not to become a national flashpoint, a workable relationship with the police, and her mother being arrested twenty-six times without being beaten, set against a cruel incident in which someone threw a chimpanzee on her, small as she was then, while she was picketing.

title: She Read the Congressional Record
summary: Asked where her mother's political skill came from, Hildreth answers all of the above: Clara read the Congressional Record as a hobby, knew everyone in Oklahoma, and became the voice of people whose problems had become struggles; she adds that her parents divorced amid the movement, her fun-loving electrician father unable to handle the pressure.

title: A Whirlwind Effect
topic: Years of Protest and Its Legacy
summary: Hildreth remembers Saturdays of her youth spent protesting, with amusement parks closed to them and a closeness among the young participants outsiders cannot grasp; recalling the standing-room 50th-anniversary commemoration of the 1958 sit-in, she says they only wanted social change in Oklahoma City but set off a whirlwind that changed American history.

title: You Can't Out-Read Mom
summary: Hildreth recounts joining her mother's 1972 US Senate primary campaign, an unbeatable debater versed in government and education from decades of study who carried the same message to tiny all-white towns across Oklahoma and surprised people of every background; they lost the primary, but with a little more time, Hildreth believes, Clara could have made historic change.

title: So Goes One, So Goes All
topic: The Sanitation Strike and a Closing Creed
summary: Hildreth closes on the 1969 Oklahoma City sanitation workers' strike, where Clara was chosen spokesperson as an honest woman of integrity who would fight to the end for people who could not represent themselves, and on the daily creed her mother taught: to believe in the rain when it does not fall, the sun when it does not shine, and a God you have never seen.
